perturbation.original_text: We will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its financial advisor, J.P. Morgan & Co. Incorporated ("JPM"), or the Company's other representatives furnish to us in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by us, or by our representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or our review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials").
perturbation.changed_text: We will treat with reasonable care any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its financial advisor, J.P. Morgan & Co. Incorporated ("JPM"), or the Company's other representatives furnish to us in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by us, or by our representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or our review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials").
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'confidentially' with 'reasonable care' weakens the obligation to protect sensitive information. Confidentiality implies a higher standard of protection than reasonable care under New York law, the state governing this agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law related to data security breaches implicitly requires more than 'reasonable care' for certain types of information. Using 'reasonable care' in this contract could be interpreted as a lower standard than required for protecting personal information under New York law, especially considering the type of data that is transferred as part of a potential acquisition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Consolidated Laws, General Business Law - GBS § 899-aa | FindLaw New York Consolidated Laws, General Business Law - GBS § 899-aa. Notification; person without valid authorization has acquired private information Current as of January 01, 2024 | Updated by FindLaw Staff 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) “ Personal information ” shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) “ Private information ” shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identification card number; (3) account number, credit or debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, password or other information that would permit access to an individual's financial account; (4) account number, credit or debit card number, if circumstances exist wherein such number could be used to access an individual's financial account without additional identifying information, security code, access code, or password; or (5) biometric information, meaning data generated by electronic measurements of an individual's unique physical characteristics, such as a fingerprint, voice print, retina or iris image, or other unique physical representation or digital representation of biometric data which are used to authenticate or ascertain the individual's identity; or (ii) a user name or e-mail address in combination with a password or security question and answer that would permit access to an online account. “Private information” does not include publicly available information which is lawfully made available to the
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'confidentiality' when handling sensitive information, while the changed text only requires 'reasonable care.' This weakens the obligation to protect the information. New York General Business Law § 899-aa defines 'private information,' which includes sensitive data like social security numbers, driver's license numbers, and financial account details. The 'Law Explanation' states that New York law implicitly requires more than 'reasonable care' for data security. Therefore, replacing 'confidentiality' with 'reasonable care' could be interpreted as a lower standard than what is required by New York law for protecting personal information, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The term "Evaluation Materials" includes information furnished to us orally or in writing (whatever the form or storage medium) or gathered by inspection, and regardless of whether such information is specifically identified as "confidential".
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Evaluation Materials" includes information furnished to us orally or in writing (whatever the form or storage medium) or gathered by inspection, but only if such information is explicitly identified as "confidential" by the company.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration significantly narrows the definition of 'Evaluation Materials' to only include explicitly labelled confidential data. It contradicts the common understanding and legal interpretation that information can be confidential even without explicit marking.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets regardless of whether they are explicitly marked as confidential. Limiting the definition of 'Evaluation Materials' to only explicitly labelled information undermines the protection of trade secrets that may not be formally marked but are clearly confidential in nature and scope.

perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Evaluation Materials' to only include information explicitly labeled as "confidential." According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, this undermines the protection of trade secrets that may not be formally marked but are clearly confidential. While the provided scraped snippets do not explicitly define trade secrets, the Law Explanation states that the UTSA protects trade secrets regardless of explicit marking. Therefore, by requiring explicit labeling, the changed text contradicts the principle of protecting unmarked but confidential trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction related to misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials after a reasonable amount of time for archival purposes, retaining copies deemed necessary for internal record-keeping.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a clause allowing for retention of copies 'deemed necessary,' the modified text weakens the obligation to return or destroy Evaluation Materials. This introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with trade secret law, as it's unclear what criteria will determine necessity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. Retaining copies of Evaluation Materials, even for internal record-keeping, could be construed as misappropriation or increase the risk thereof, especially if the definition of what is 'necessary' is not clearly defined, thus conflicting with the spirit of the DTSA.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by allowing the retention of copies of 'Evaluation Materials' deemed necessary for internal record-keeping, thereby weakening the obligation to return or destroy all such materials. This contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of trade secret protection laws, as highlighted by the LLM's explanation, which mentions the potential for misappropriation under the DTSA. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 only refers to 'Civil proceedings' related to violations of trade secrets, the modification introduces ambiguity about what is considered 'necessary', increasing the risk of misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: We understand and agree that in cases of a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives, the Company will be entitled to seek money damages. Specific performance and injunctive relief may be considered if monetary compensation is deemed inadequate by the breaching party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that money damages are insufficient and that the company is entitled to specific performance. The altered clause gives priority to money damages and grants the breaching party power to decide when specific performance is needed. This could conflict with the understanding of equitable remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Equity Law - Specific Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Specific performance is typically granted when monetary damages are inadequate, especially in contracts involving unique assets or confidential information. This modification gives the breaching party undue influence over whether this equitable remedy is available, potentially contradicting established legal principles governing specific performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original agreement regarding remedies for breach. The original text prioritizes specific performance and injunctive relief, stating money damages are insufficient. The changed text reverses this, prioritizing money damages and granting the breaching party the power to decide if specific performance is needed, undermining the equitable remedy. The Wex snippet states that specific performance is used when "monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach". By giving the breaching party control over this decision, the modified text contradicts the established legal principle where specific performance is considered when monetary damages are insufficient. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where the contract deviates from legally recognized definitions concerning equitable remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, including its conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly including the 'conflicts of law principles' of New York, the agreement introduces uncertainty. The original wording purposefully excluded conflicts of law to ensure New York law directly applies to the interpretation of the contract. Including conflicts of law could result in another state's laws governing the contract, despite the intent to apply New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose a governing law, New York General Obligations Law provides specific rules regarding choice of law, especially when the transaction is significant. The original clause aimed to bypass these rules for certainty. Re-introducing the conflict of laws principles could then invoke said NY GOL rules, which might in turn dictate the contract follow laws outside of the jurisdiction of New York state, thus undermining the original choice of law clause.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 (2022) - Choice of Law. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GOB - General Obligations Article 5 - Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations Title 14 - Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum in Certain Transactions 5-1401 - Choice of Law. Universal Citation: NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. perturbation.justification: The inclusion of 'conflicts of law principles' in the changed text contradicts the original intent to apply New York law directly, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation also supports this, noting that re-introducing conflict of laws principles might cause the contract to follow laws outside of New York. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing/invalid, Scraped Law Snippet 2 (NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401) suggests that parties can choose the law of New York to govern their rights and duties. The original text ensured this choice was straightforward. The changed text introduces uncertainty, potentially undermining this initial choice and creating a contradiction of law.
